topic: A person's online friend turns out to live in the same city
raw_output:
[story 1]

The blue light of my screen was the only illumination in my cramped apartment. I scrolled through Maya’s feed, a cascade of perfectly curated city snapshots. She was always somewhere exciting—galleries, rooftop bars, bustling markets. I watched her laugh in a photo taken near the riverfront, a sound that always seemed miles away from my own quiet street. A tightness began to coil in my chest, a dull, heavy pressure that made my breath catch. I traced the outline of her profile, the vibrant energy emanating from her posts feeling like a physical ache. Then, a notification popped up: "Guess where I am?" My heart gave a sudden, erratic thump against my ribs. I typed a hesitant reply, my fingers trembling slightly. The next message arrived, and the world tilted on its axis. "Downtown, near the old clock tower. You?" My jaw went slack. The realization hit me with the force of a physical blow. She lived just three blocks from my building.

[story 2]

I spent the evening meticulously crafting a witty comment for Maya’s latest post, trying to inject some spark into the digital exchange. My usual carefully constructed facade felt brittle tonight. Every time I looked at the photos—the vibrant street art, the neon glow reflecting off wet pavement—a strange, hot feeling bloomed low in my stomach. It wasn't satisfaction; it was a sharp, unwelcome comparison. I felt a distinct urge to check my own surroundings, to measure my dull reality against her vivid existence. Then, a direct message pinged. It was from her. "Hey, random coincidence, but I think we might be in the same city." My hand froze halfway through typing. The silence in the room became deafening. I stared at the screen, my gaze darting from the notification back to the window, suddenly hyper-aware of the familiar skyline outside.

[story 3]

I replayed the conversation in my head, the digital banter feeling suddenly hollow. Maya’s descriptions of her weekend—the cafes she frequented, the parks she explored—painted a picture of a life I felt completely excluded from. A slow burn started in my core, a simmering resentment that had been building for weeks, now stoked by this unexpected proximity. I started pacing the small space between my desk and the window, my movements jerky. I felt a distinct tightening around my eyes, a kind of internal constriction. When the message finally landed—a simple location pin—I physically recoiled, knocking over a stack of books. I leaned back against the wall, my breathing shallow, trying to process the sheer improbability of it all. She was *here*. And I was stuck right beside her.
